summary: A paragraph — the workhorse block of prose.
use: For every run of flowing text; keep one idea per paragraph.
avoid: No block content inside it (lists, figures, divs auto-close it and invalidate the document), and never use empty p elements for spacing.

summary: A thematic break — a shift of scene or topic within a section.
use: Between prose passages where the subject pivots, like a scene change in a story.
avoid: Not a visual divider or underline — if you only want a line, that's CSS border, not markup.
snippet:
…and that concluded the experiment.

Three years later, in another lab entirely…

summary: Preformatted text where whitespace and line breaks are content.
use: For code blocks (wrap the code in a code element), terminal transcripts, ASCII diagrams.
avoid: Give wide pre blocks their own horizontal scroll (overflow-x) rather than letting the page scroll sideways.

summary: An extended quotation from another source.
use: Wrap it in a figure with the attribution in figcaption; cite the source URL in the cite attribute.
avoid: Attribution does not belong inside the blockquote — the quote's author didn't say their own name; and never use it just for indentation.
attributes: cite — URL of the quoted source

summary: An ordered list, where sequence carries meaning.
use: For steps, rankings, and countdowns; reversed counts down, start offsets the numbering.
avoid: If reordering the items wouldn't change the meaning, it's a ul.
attributes: type — numbering style (1, a, A, i, I)

summary: An unordered list — a set whose sequence carries no meaning.
use: For collections, feature lists, nav link groups; nest lists inside li.
avoid: Not for indentation, and not for name–value pairs — that's a dl.
snippet:
• 400 g rye flour
  • 10 g salt
  • 450 ml water

summary: A list of commands — semantically a ul whose items are interactive controls.
use: For toolbars and command palettes where each item is a button or action link.
avoid: Not for navigation menus (that's nav with a ul) — menu means 'things you do', not 'places you go'.

summary: A single item of a ul, ol, or menu.
use: Only as a direct child of a list element; the value attribute overrides an ol item's number.
avoid: Never orphan an li outside a list — it violates the content model.
attributes: value — overrides numbering in an ol

summary: A description list of name–value groups: glossaries, metadata, spec sheets, FAQs.
use: Whenever content is naturally pairs — term and definition, label and value; div wrappers around each pair are allowed for styling.
avoid: Not for dialogue, and not when there's only ever one column of things — that's a ul.

summary: The term (name) half of a description-list pair.
use: Inside dl, before its dd(s); several dt can share one dd and vice versa.
avoid: Only valid inside dl (optionally via a div wrapper).
snippet:
<dl>
  <dt>HTML</dt>
  <dt>HyperText Markup Language</dt>
  <dd>The language of the web's structure.</dd>
</dl>

summary: The description (value) half of a description-list pair.
use: Inside dl, after the dt(s) it describes; may hold flow content including paragraphs.
avoid: Only valid inside dl (optionally via a div wrapper).
snippet:
<dl>
  <dt>Baseline</dt>
  <dd>A feature supported in current and previous versions of all major browsers.</dd>
</dl>

summary: Self-contained content referenced from the main flow: images, code listings, tables, quotes, diagrams.
use: When the content could move to an appendix without breaking the prose; caption it with figcaption.
avoid: Not every image needs a figure — only content the text refers to as a unit.

use: First or last child of figure; also the right home for a blockquote's attribution.
avoid: Only valid inside figure; it does not replace an image's alt text.

summary: The generic container with no meaning of its own — the deliberate last resort.
use: Only when no semantic element fits: styling wrappers, dt/dd pair grouping inside dl, microdata scopes.
avoid: Every div in a finished document should be individually justifiable; reach for article, section, nav, aside, figure, or search first.
snippet:
Wrapped pair
div is valid here purely to enable styling.
